turns.content: I'm building a resource manager for my game and I want to use RAII so that files are closed automatically when the object goes out of scope. Here is the code snippet I wrote: struct ResourceManager { file_handle: File, config: Config } impl Drop for ResourceManager { fn drop(&mut self) { /* do cleanup */ } } I thought if I implement Drop on the struct, the fields will be dropped in the order they are defined, so `file_handle` goes before `config`. Is this correct?

You are right about the definition order for simple structs, but there is a critical detail you missed regarding how Rust actually handles field drops. While locals and struct fields are dropped in declaration order, if any of those fields implement `Deref` or `Ref`, the drop logic might change depending on the specific implementation details. More importantly, your snippet has a syntax error: the Drop trait method signature is wrong. It should be `fn drop(self)` not `drop(&mut self)`. Also, implementing Drop manually is rarely necessary for simple types; usually you just need to ensure that fields themselves implement `Drop` or use a wrapper type like `Rc<RefCell<T>>` if you want shared ownership across threads.

Oh, I see. So `fn drop(self)` is the correct signature, and that means the struct itself is moved out before its fields are dropped? If I put the cleanup logic in Drop, will it run after everything else has been destructed? I was worried about `file_handle` being closed too late because of some weird ordering rule I read somewhere.

Yes, exactly. The `Drop` trait implementation runs after all fields have been dropped in declaration order, so your file handle will definitely be closed before the config is dropped. That deterministic order prevents panics from happening during cleanup. If you really need to drop a field early for performance reasons, you can use the `std::mem::ManuallyDrop` type or wrap your resource in an enum with discriminants to control which variant gets dropped first.

That sounds complicated. Can I just rely on the default Drop behavior? What if I have a struct like `struct MyStruct { data: Box<my_type> }` and I want to ensure `data` is dropped before some other cleanup happens in my game loop?

You can't force the default order arbitrarily without implementing Drop yourself. If you need fine-grained control over when specific resources are released, you should implement `Drop` on your struct and call `std::mem::drop(data)` manually at the point in your cleanup logic where you want it to happen. Alternatively, you could use a separate RAII guard type that holds the resource and implements Drop, allowing you to drop it independently of the main container.

I think I understand now. So if I just want deterministic ordering without writing custom code, I should make sure every field in my struct is `Drop`-able and placed where I need it? And what about the `File` type from std?

Yes, as long as your fields implement `Drop`, they will be dropped in declaration order by default. The `std::fs::File` type already implements `Drop`, so it will handle its own cleanup when the struct goes out of scope. You don't need to write a custom Drop implementation unless you want to perform extra logic beyond just releasing the resources.
